goal_03.config.title: Maternal mortality
goal_03.content.#0.text: SDG 3 goal: Ensure healthy lives and well-being for all ages​
goal_03.content.#1.text: Target 3.8: Achieve universal health coverage, including financial
goal_03.content.#2.text: risk protection, access to quality essential health-care services and
goal_03.content.#3.text: access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines
goal_03.content.#5.text: Without UHC → fragmented progress and inequity​
goal_03.content.#6.text: Maternal and child health: core outcomes reflecting system strength
goal_03.content.#7.title: UHC Service Coverage
goal_03.content.#7.title_scene_1: UCH Service Coverage Index by regions
goal_03.content.#7.title_scene_2: UCH Service Coverage Index by sub-components
goal_03.content.#7.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: The global UHC SCI score ]steadily increased from 45 to 68 between 2000 and 2021, but the progress has slowed since 2015
goal_03.content.#7.scenes.#1.text: [emphasis: The UHC Service Coverage Index] is defined as coverage of essential services (including RMNCH, infectious diseases, NCDs, capacity & access).​
goal_03.content.#8.text: Inequalities in health service coverage remain despite global progress​
goal_03.content.#9.title: Global UHC Service Coverage Index
goal_03.content.#11.text: Maternal & child health outcomes in the UHC framework
goal_03.content.#12.text: Target 3.1: By 2030, reduce the global maternal mortality ratio to less than 70
goal_03.content.#14.text: Target 3.2: By 2030, end preventable deaths of newborns and children under 5
goal_03.content.#15.text: years of age, with all countries aiming to reduce neonatal mortality to at least
goal_03.content.#16.text: as low as 12 per 1,000 live births and under-5 mortality to at least as low as 25
goal_03.content.#18.text: Linked to UHC monitoring: coverage of maternal and child health services is a key sub-index in the UHC framework.​
goal_03.content.#19.text: Proof of progress: falling mortality indicates better access to life-saving care.​
goal_03.content.#20.text: System quality: high deaths reveal gaps in timely, affordable, quality care​
goal_03.content.#21.text: Interlinked outcomes: maternal and child survival are closely connected — the loss of a
goal_03.content.#22.text: mother greatly increases a child’s risk of dying.
goal_03.content.#23.title: Maternal Mortality vs Neonatal Mortality (2020)
goal_03.content.#25.text: Levels and trends in maternal mortality​
goal_03.content.#26.title: Levels and trends in maternal mortality
goal_03.content.#26.scenes.#0.text: Overall [emphasis: maternal mortality​ rate] of 197 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births in 2023​. Despite major reductions since 2000, the pace of decline has stagnated in recent years, with a temporary uptick duringCOVID-19 before returning to previous trends.[emphasis: ]
goal_03.content.#26.scenes.#1.text: The global [emphasis: under-5 mortality rate] has halved from 77 in 2000 to 37 deaths per 1,000 live births in 2023, saving millions of young lives. ​ Despite progress, Sub-Saharan Africa still records mortality rates (68) more than 10 times higher than high-income countries (5). Progress in reducing neonatal deaths has been slower — 17 per 1,000 live births in 2023, making newborns an increasing share of child mortality.​[emphasis: ]
goal_03.content.#26.scenes.#2.text: In terms of the [emphasis: neonatal mortality rate ]South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa still face the highest burdens, though both regions show steady improvement.
goal_03.content.#28.text: While some countries like Sierra Leone and Ethiopia in Sub-Saharan Africa made
goal_03.content.#29.text: faster progress, many countries in Latin America & the Caribbean regressedin reducing maternal mortality.
goal_03.content.#30.title: Country speed scores
goal_03.content.#30.scenes.#0.text: While some countries like Sierra Leone and Ethiopia in Sub-Saharan Africa made faster progress, many countries in Latin America & the Caribbean regressed in reducing maternal mortality.
goal_03.content.#30.scenes.#1.text: Rwanda and Malawi in Sub-Saharan Africa, along with several countries in Asia and the Pacific, advanced quickly, but many Sub-Saharan African countries still show slower declines in under-5 mortality.
goal_03.content.#30.scenes.#2.text: Cambodia, China, and India in Asia and the Pacific and South Asia made faster gains, yet Sub-Saharan Africa shows uneven improvements in reducing
goal_03.content.#32.text: Common Factors Behind Progress – studies to be cited​
goal_03.content.#38.text: RMNCH service is a core component of the UHC SCI.​
goal_03.content.#39.text: Countries that increased overall UHC (e.g., Sierra Leone, Ethiopia, Bangladesh) also show progress in maternal mortality reduction.​
goal_03.content.#40.text: Service coverage helps ensure essential RMNCH care is available and accessible.

goal_03.content.#41.title_scene_3: Neonatal Mortality Rate vs UHC Index
goal_03.content.#41.scenes.#1.text: Countries that increased overall UHC (e.g., Rwanda, Cambodia, India and China) also show progress in [emphasis: child mortality reduction].

goal_03.content.#46.text: UHC is critical to sustaining maternal and child health gains and ensuring
goal_03.content.#48.text: Integrating health service and financing policies is key to expanding both coverage and affordability​
goal_03.content.#49.text: Monitoring SDG 3.1, 3.2, and 3.8 together keeps attention on survival, access, and protection—and accelerates progress to 2030
